schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott fix the Hebrew orders and setting: the anointing runs while Joram lies ill, and the flight is to avoid entanglement, not danger.
Henry explains why Elisha delegates the errand (age, danger, a young man's strength) and rejects an earlier secret anointing by Elijah.
Gill notes the tradition that the messenger was Jonah and stresses that Jehu neither sought nor expected the throne.
Clarke reports the Jewish identification of the young prophet as Jonah and the urgency of the charge to gird up his loins.

debates.question: Had Jehu already been anointed privately by Elijah, as David was by Samuel?

sides.position: No; God reserved the act for Elisha to perform after Elijah's departure
Some suppose an earlier anointing

schools.summary: Ellicott and Keil read 'enter into it' as proof Ramoth was retaken, gloss 'chamber in a chamber,' and adduce Jehu's name on Shalmaneser's Assyrian monuments.
Gill notes only that Jehu was to be sought out at Ramoth, from a school of the prophets nearby.

debates.question: Was Ramoth-gilead under siege or already in Israelite hands?

sides.position: Retaken and held by Israel, as 'enter into it' and the later orders imply
Josephus relates a siege

schools.summary: Ellicott notes only the chief part of the charge is given here, weighs the 'over/unto Israel' variant, and reads 'tarry not' as giving force to the act.
Gill supplies the assumed circumstance: the oil is poured in the chamber, alone.
Gill reads the emphatic 'young man' to his honour — bold, less known, and himself a prophet, not merely a disciple.
Ellicott finds the construction unusual and notes MSS/versions that smooth it; the point is textual, not doctrinal.
Clarke would render 'the servant of the prophet' — Elisha's minister who succeeded Gehazi.

debates.question: Is he 'the young man the prophet' or 'the servant of the prophet'?

sides.position: A prophet himself, though young
Elisha's servant/minister (a textual variant)

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott trace the action step by step: the captains in council, Jehu singled out and drawn indoors for the private anointing.
Gill supplies the setting — the captains at dinner or a council of war.
Ellicott and Keil set the anointing as the fulfilment of Elijah's commission (1 Kings 19:16) and rebut Reuss's charge that Elisha was a regicide.
Clarke reads 'king over the people of the LORD' as a charge to rule by God's law and restore true worship.
Gill notes Jehu enters the inner chamber to receive the anointing.

debates.question: Is the anointing a genuine divine commission or a later fabrication to legitimise Jehu's dynasty?

sides.position: A true fulfilment of the commission given to Elijah
A substitution for a lost Elijah-document; Elisha a revolutionary

schools.summary: Wesley and Clarke fix the ground of judgment as persecution — the blood of the prophets — and the forfeit of life by the shedder of blood (Genesis 9:6).
Gill: the act would be treason but for the express warrant of the King of kings.
Ellicott sharpens 'the house of Ahab' and supplies the history of Jezebel's persecution.
Ellicott expounds the coarse idiom 'him that is shut up and left' as meaning all without exception, and refers the language back to 1 Kings 21:21.
Gill: the whole house cut off by violent death, none escaping.

themes: the patience and certainty of justice

schools.summary: Keil marks the verse as a repetition of 1 Kings 21:21-23; Ellicott supplies the parallels for Baasha and Jeroboam and an Assyrian note on the name Ba'sa.
Gill treats the verse as a cross-reference to the same threat already uttered.
Clarke marvels how minutely the prophecy of Jezebel's end by the dogs was fulfilled (vv.33-37).
Ellicott gives the literal Hebrew order and refers the threat to Elijah's oracle in 1 Kings 21:23.
Gill notes the threat is already spoken and that the young prophet then flees.
Henry: the world's scorn of the prophet as 'mad' is the same contempt cast on Christ, John, and Paul; the godly are counted mad by those who are mad indeed.
Clarke and Wesley turn the sneer against the profane soldiers who scoff at holy men while pretending to fight for religion.
Keil explains meshugga as derision of prophetic ecstasy; Ellicott finds two readings of Jehu's guarded 'Ye know the man.'

debates.question: What does Jehu mean by 'Ye know the man, and his communication'?

sides.position: A hint that the officers themselves had planned it — probing their disposition
Simply 'why ask me, when you can guess?' — deliberate concealment

themes: the world calls the godly mad

schools.summary: Ellicott and Keil refine the one-word reply — 'trickery!' / 'thou dost not speak truth' — and note the officers' insistence assured Jehu he could trust them.
Gill reconstructs the clues that fed the captains' curiosity and their importunate pressing.
Clarke gives the Chaldee 'Thou liest' with a gentler paraphrase.
Henry notes the real risk Jehu ran in disclosing his anointing.

debates.question: What is the force of the captains' reply, 'It is false'?

sides.position: 'Thou liest' — a flat contradiction
'Trickery! you are pretending' — a charge of evasion, not falsehood

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott debate gerem hammaaloth ('bare steps' / 'body of the stairs') and read the army's instant homage as proof of strong feeling against Joram.
Clarke and Wesley read the spread garments as acknowledgement of Jehu's authority, echoing the homage to Christ (Matthew 21:7).
Gill favours the sundial reading and notes the garments were carpets or tapestry, not wearing apparel.

debates.question: What is 'the top of the stairs' (gerem hammaaloth)?

sides.position: A public sundial of graduated steps
'Upon the bare steps themselves'
'On to the body of the stairs' — the rooftop stairway as platform

schools.summary: Keil and Clarke show from the parenthesis that Israel held Ramoth against Hazael, so 'kept' means garrison not siege; the surprise depends on sealing the city.
Gill states the plain fact of the confederacy to depose Joram.

themes: war as the door of judgment

schools.summary: Ellicott makes 'let not a fugitive go forth' decisive proof that Israel held Ramoth (against Josephus's siege), and savours the deferential 'if it be your soul.'
Gill notes the repeated notice of Joram's wounding fixes the setting.

debates.question: Was Ramoth besieged (Josephus) or held by Israel?

sides.position: Held by Israel — else the command to let no fugitive out is unintelligible
Besieged, as Josephus relates

schools.summary: Ellicott sets Ahaziah's visit (2 Kings 8:29) and untangles a Septuagint interpolation carried over from the previous verse.
Henry supplies the geography and the providence: men hasten to meet their ruin when their day to fall has come.
Gill pictures Jehu riding in royal pomp toward Jezreel.
Ellicott reads shiph'ah as a 'considerable force' and argues Joram's 'Is it peace?' means only 'What is the news?'; Clarke describes the watchman-telegraph.
Gill explains the watchman's heightened vigilance because the king was present.

debates.question: What does Joram's 'Is it peace?' express?

sides.position: Unsuspecting — merely 'What is the news?' from the army
'Come ye with friendly or hostile intention?'

schools.summary: Ellicott reads Jehu's 'What hast thou to do with peace?' as a rough evasion meant to prevent alarm; Keil parses the taunt and 'turn behind me.'
Gill notes the messenger was detained, as the next would be.
Ellicott reads the second report as a question 'Is it peace?' not a salutation, showing Joram still unsuspicious, assuming mere accident.
Gill notes the second rider fared exactly as the first.
Ellicott links 'furiously' to the word for 'mad fellow' and notes the Targum/Josephus opposite reading 'quietly'; Clarke calls Jehu's driving proverbial.
Henry balances the lesson: God fits the violent man to the task, yet 'he that has rule over his own spirit is better than the mighty.'

sides.position: Furiously — the natural sense, tied to the word for madness
Quietly / slowly, in orderly fashion

schools.summary: Ellicott and Clarke note the kings ride out unsuspecting and unguarded, and place Naboth's vineyard within the palace grounds.
Wesley draws the governing principle: providence orders events so the punishment answers the sin as face answers face in a glass.
Clarke reads the 'whoredoms' as literal impure religion; Wesley allows both literal and spiritual, and presses 'the way of sin can never be the way of peace.'
Ellicott takes the whoredoms as spiritual idolatries and witchcrafts as Semitic sorceries, casting Jehu as champion of legitimate worship.
Gill glosses the question as asking whether Jehu comes in triumph or in hostility.

sides.position: Literal — she patronised an impure system of religion
Spiritual — idolatry called whoredom
Both together

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott parse Joram's wheeling flight and his two-word cry 'mirmah!' (treachery); Clarke notes his concern even now for Ahaziah.
Gill pictures the physical turning of the reins as Joram flees.
Clarke, Ellicott, and Keil fix the idiom 'filled his hand with the bow' and the oblique wound between the shoulders through the heart.
Wesley calls the arrow 'one of God's arrows, which he ordained against the persecutor' (Psalms 7:13).
Henry: Joram, the top branch of Ahab's house, is first cut off, dying a criminal under the sentence of the law.
Ellicott and Keil explain Bidkar ('son of stabbing'), the shared ride behind Ahab, and 'burden' (massa) as a threatening prophetic oracle.
Gill clarifies that Bidkar was now Jehu's captain, once Ahab's and Joram's.
Ellicott explains the 'sons' and 'yesterday' as eye-witness details, and finds in the prophetic diction ('burden,' 'uttered of Jehovah') proof of the oracle's authenticity.
Clarke argues Naboth's sons were slain too, to secure the inheritance beyond reclaiming.

debates.question: Why does Jehu name Naboth's 'sons,' unmentioned in 1 Kings 21?

sides.position: They were slain with him to secure the confiscated inheritance
An eye-witness detail (Jehu's own version) consonant with ancient practice

schools.summary: Ellicott traces the flight to Gur, Ibleam, and Megiddo, and prefers to suspect a textual corruption over forced harmonization with 2 Chronicles 22.
Clarke (quoting Lightfoot) and Wesley reconcile Kings and Chronicles by combining: hidden in Samaria, caught, brought to Jehu, slain at Megiddo.
Henry: Ahaziah dies because joined to Ahab's house in affinity and iniquity — a warning against associating with evil-doers.

debates.question: How are the differing accounts of Ahaziah's death (Kings vs 2 Chronicles 22) to be reconciled?

sides.position: Harmonize by combining the narratives (hidden in Samaria, then slain at Megiddo)
The localities here are more trustworthy; suspect textual corruption rather than force a harmony

outliers.note: Supplies a detailed chronological harmony of Kings and Chronicles for the movements and death of Ahaziah.

schools.summary: Gill: Ahaziah is buried by Jehu's leave because he was the grandson of the godly Jehoshaphat.
Ellicott notes the king was laid in his own prepared sepulchre and that the Hebrew requires a supplied 'and brought him.'

themes: mercy for a pious ancestor's sake

schools.summary: Ellicott ascribes the eleventh/twelfth-year difference to the loose synchronisms of the two kingdoms, and thinks the verse a parenthesis, perhaps spurious.
Gill resolves it by the turn of the year or a co-regency with his father.
Clarke reads a viceroyalty in Joram's sickness followed by sole reign in the twelfth year.

debates.question: Why 'eleventh year' here but 'twelfth' in 2 Kings 8:25?

sides.position: A co-regency, then sole reign
Loose synchronisms / differing computations; verse perhaps a gloss

schools.summary: Henry reads the paint as defiance meant to daunt Jehu, and the unhumbled heart under humbling providences as the surest presage of ruin (with the Thyatiran Jezebel, Revelation 2).
Ellicott: she adorned herself to die as a queen (like Cleopatra), and rejects Ewald's notion that she meant to seduce Jehu; Clarke details the antimony kohl.

debates.question: Why did Jezebel paint her face and adorn her head?

sides.position: To intimidate Jehu and stop his career
To die with royal dignity, as a queen
To captivate the conqueror by her charms

schools.summary: Henry: Jezebel misapplies the precedent of Zimri, ignoring that Jehu acts by divine warrant, not self-willed ambition; we must distinguish the precious from the vile.
Ellicott reads the taunt as ironical, branding Jehu a second short-reigned Zimri (1 Kings 16:15-18); Clarke reports the range of renderings.
Wesley: a taunt that Zimri had brief enjoyment of his treason.

debates.question: Is 'Had Zimri peace?' a conciliation or an ironical threat?

sides.position: A bitter, ironical threat branding Jehu a doomed regicide
Words meant rather to conciliate (Jarchi)

schools.summary: Ellicott: Jehu disdains to answer Jezebel and appeals to the eunuchs, who hated her and would gladly turn on their former tyrant; the doubled cry fits his character.
Gill notes Jehu lifted his face on hearing her taunt.
Keil and Ellicott describe the throwing-down and settle the grammar — 'he trode' means Jehu drove over her body; Ewald overreaches in saying he spurned her with his feet.
Clarke recoils at the terrible, brutal death.
Henry: vengeance for the stoning of Naboth — the manner answers the crime; the Lord is righteous.
Ellicott sees Jehu eat before recalling Jezebel's royal rank, with perhaps a touch of remorse; Keil glosses 'cursed woman' as smitten by God's curse; Clarke lists her lineage.
Wesley: Jehu says 'king's daughter' not 'king's wife' to show no respect to Ahab's house, and had forgotten the charge of v.10.
Keil states plainly that only skull, feet, and palms remained, the dogs having eaten and dragged away the rest — the sentence fulfilled while Jehu dined.
Clarke marvels how literally Elijah's prediction was fulfilled and reports (without endorsing) the rabbinic fancy about the spared parts.

themes: the word runs ahead of Jehu

schools.summary: Ellicott weighs 'portion' (heleq) here against 'wall' (hel) in 1 Kings 21:23 as a scribal slip, contra Keil who takes them as distinct; both read the deed as fulfilment of Elijah's word.
Gill records the report and Jehu's recognition of the fulfilled prophecy.

debates.question: Is 'portion' (heleq) here versus 'wall' (hel) in 1 Kings 21:23 a real difference?

sides.position: A copyist's slip from a lost final letter — not original
A genuine difference of wording

schools.summary: Clarke: literally fulfilled — no tomb, no stone, no memorial; judgment is God's strange but terrible work.
Ellicott holds the closing clause probably original (against Keil's 'free expansion' and Luther's 'historian's remark') and fixes its sense: her remains unrecognisable.
Henry: her name survives only as stigmatized in Scripture — the name of the wicked shall rot above ground.

debates.question: Is the closing clause part of Elijah's oracle, Jehu's expansion, or the historian's remark?

sides.position: Probably original to the prophecy
A free expansion by Jehu
A closing remark of the historian
